title: Can You Combine NECO and WAEC Results for University Admission?
author: Tklesson Team
content:
You have your WAEC results and your NECO results. Between the two of them, you have at least five credit passes, including English and Mathematics. But they are not all on the same certificate. Your WAEC has a credit in English but a pass in Mathematics. Your NECO has a credit in Mathematics but a pass in English. Can you combine them to meet university admission requirements? The short answer is yes, in most cases. But the rules are not the same for every institution and every course. Here is how combining results works and what you need to know before you apply.

The General Rule: Two Sittings Are Accepted

Most Nigerian universities, polytechnics, and colleges of education accept results from two examination sittings. This means you can present credits from one WAEC sitting and one NECO sitting, or from two WAEC sittings, or from two NECO sittings. The key requirement is that all five credit passes, including English Language and Mathematics, must be earned across no more than two examination attempts. A student with credits spread across three or more sittings may be at a disadvantage, as many institutions explicitly state that results must be from no more than two sittings.

The logic behind the two-sitting rule is straightforward. Universities want to see that you can perform consistently. A student who earns all five credits in one sitting has demonstrated the ability to handle a full academic load simultaneously. A student who needs three or four sittings to accumulate five credits raises questions about their ability to manage the demands of university coursework. The two-sitting allowance provides flexibility without removing the expectation of competence. You get a second chance, but not unlimited chances.

How to Present Combined Results in Your Application

When you apply through JAMB, you will be asked to enter your O'Level results. This is where you list your WAEC and NECO subjects and grades. Enter all your results from both sittings. The system will automatically consider the best grade for each subject. If your WAEC English is a C6 and your NECO English is a B3, the system will use the B3. You do not need to choose which result to submit for each subject. Submit both certificates and let the system or the admissions office select the best combination.

During post-UTME screening or physical verification, you will be asked to present your original certificates or result slips. Bring both your WAEC and NECO results. The university will verify that the grades you entered online match the grades on your certificates. Any discrepancy between what you entered and what your certificates show will disqualify you, even if your actual grades are strong enough for admission. Accuracy in data entry is non-negotiable. Double-check every grade before submitting your application.

Institutions That May Not Accept Combined Results

While most institutions accept combined results, some competitive programs at top universities prefer or require results from a single sitting. This is especially true for courses like Medicine, Law, and Engineering at federal universities with high application volumes. The official requirement may state that two sittings are accepted, but in practice, when there are more qualified applicants than available spaces, the university will prioritize candidates with single-sitting results. A student with five credits in one sitting is more competitive than a student with the same grades across two sittings.

Some private universities also prefer single-sitting results as part of their admissions criteria, though they are typically more flexible than public institutions. Always check the specific requirements of your target institutions. The JAMB brochure, updated annually, lists the O'Level requirements for every course at every Nigerian university. If a course says one sitting only, do not apply with combined results. You will be disqualified at the verification stage.

Strategic Implications for Current Students

If you are still in secondary school and have not yet taken WAEC or NECO, the best strategy is to aim for five credits including English and Mathematics in a single sitting. Treat the first exam as your primary attempt, not as a trial run. Prepare as if you only have one chance, because for the most competitive courses, you effectively do.

If you have already taken one exam and did not earn all the credits you need, focus your preparation for the second exam on the specific subjects where you fell short. You do not need to retake subjects you already passed. The combined result approach is designed for targeted improvement, not for retaking everything. Use it strategically.

The Bottom Line

Most Nigerian universities accept combined WAEC and NECO results across a maximum of two examination sittings. When applying, enter all your results from both sittings and let the admissions system select the best grades. Some competitive programs prefer single-sitting results, so check the specific requirements for your target course and institution. If you are still preparing for your exams, aim for a single-sitting qualification. If you need to combine results, use the second sitting to improve specific subjects rather than retaking everything. The two-sitting allowance is a useful flexibility, but it should be a backup plan, not your primary strategy.
